toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Your Agent Skills Get Smarter Every Time You Forge One. Here's How.

*Inside Skill Forge's Deep tier — where compilation builds a searchable knowledge base, automatically.*

---

## The Intelligence Gap

You've compiled an agent skill. It's good — AST-verified signatures, provenance citations, confidence tiers. Your agent stops hallucinating that library's API. Problem solved.

But what happens when you compile your *second* skill? Your *tenth*? When you audit a skill six weeks later and need to understand how the source has drifted?

Each skill compilation produces rich, structured data: 354 verified exports, co-import patterns, confidence ratings, provenance maps. Then that intelligence sits in files on disk — invisible to every subsequent workflow. The next audit-skill run starts from scratch. The next update-skill extraction has no memory of what came before.

The data exists. It's curated, high-confidence, structurally verified. But nothing connects it.

What if every skill you compiled made every future workflow smarter?

---

## Deep Tier: Where Compilation Builds Knowledge

Skill Forge's progressive capability model has three tiers:

- **Quick** — GitHub CLI reads source files. Best-effort extraction.
- **Forge** — ast-grep parses the Abstract Syntax Tree. Structural truth, T1 confidence.
- **Deep** — ast-grep + GitHub CLI + QMD. Everything Forge provides, plus a searchable knowledge layer that grows with every skill you create.

Quick and Forge produce excellent skills. Deep does something fundamentally different: it turns your skill compilation pipeline into a **progressive knowledge base**.

Here's what that means in practice.

---

## Progressive Collection Building

When you compile a skill in Deep tier, the workflow doesn't just write files to disk. After generating all seven artifacts — SKILL.md, context-snippet, metadata, references, provenance map, evidence report, extraction rules — it indexes the curated output into a QMD collection.

```
Artifacts generated.

Deliverables (skills/payment-service/):
- SKILL.md
- context-snippet.md
- metadata.json
- references/ (12 files)

Workspace (forge-data/payment-service/):
- provenance-map.json
- evidence-report.md
- extraction-rules.yaml

QMD collection registered: payment-service-extraction
```

That last line is the Deep tier difference. The structured, confidence-rated extraction data — every function signature, every type definition, every co-import pattern — is now searchable via QMD's hybrid search engine. Keyword matching. Semantic search. LLM-powered re-ranking.

When you brief a skill, the same thing happens:

```
Skill brief written successfully.

File: forge-data/payment-service/skill-brief.yaml
QMD collection registered: payment-service-brief
```

Every workflow step that produces an artifact enriches the searchable knowledge base as a natural side-effect of doing its job. No extra commands. No manual indexing. No configuration.

You compile skills. Deep tier builds knowledge.

---

## Why Curated Data Beats Raw Source

The intuitive approach to search is "index everything." Clone the repo, dump all source files into a search engine, let users query freely. It sounds comprehensive. It's actually noisy.

Consider a real extraction from a knowledge base framework — 197 Python source files containing infrastructure adapters, internal utilities, test helpers, `__init__.py` boilerplate, and the actual public API surface. A raw source index treats all of these equally. Search for "add data" and you get import statements, private helper functions, variable assignments, and — buried somewhere in the results — the actual public function you wanted.

Now consider what the extraction pipeline produces from those same 197 files:

```
Files scanned: ~197 unique Python files
Exports found: ~354 unique entries
  - Functions: ~120 (async functions, static methods)
  - Classes: ~140 (models, adapters, engines, handlers)
  - Types/Enums: ~30
  - Constants/Singletons: ~10
  - Data Models (Pydantic/SQLAlchemy): ~54

Confidence: 354 T1 (source-reading verified), 0 T1-low
```

Each export carries its full signature, file path, line number, parameter types, return type, and confidence tier. The extraction pipeline has already done the hard work of separating signal from noise. When this curated data gets indexed into QMD, every search returns *the right thing* — because there's no wrong thing in the collection.

Search "add data" against the extraction collection and you get:

```
async def add(data, dataset_name, ...) — ingest data
[AST:cognee/__init__.py:L42] Confidence: T1
```

One result. The right result. With provenance.

This is the core insight: **index the output of intelligence, not the input to it**.

---

## The Registry: Connective Tissue

Collections don't float in the void. Every QMD collection created by a workflow is registered in `forge-tier.yaml` — the same file that tracks tool availability and capability tier:

```yaml
tools:
  ast_grep: true
  gh_cli: true
  qmd: true

tier: Deep
tier_detected_at: "2026-03-14T10:30:00Z"

qmd_collections:
  - name: "payment-service-extraction"
    type: "extraction"
    source_workflow: "create-skill"
    skill_name: "payment-service"
    created_at: "2026-03-14"
  - name: "payment-service-brief"
    type: "brief"
    source_workflow: "brief-skill"
    skill_name: "payment-service"
    created_at: "2026-03-14"
```

Six lines per collection. The registry serves as the single source of truth for what's been indexed. Downstream workflows don't guess — they read the registry, find collections by skill name and type, and query precisely what they need.

This decoupling is deliberate. The workflow that *creates* a collection (create-skill) has no knowledge of the workflows that *consume* it (audit-skill, update-skill). They communicate through the registry. Add a new producer? Consumers discover it automatically. Add a new consumer? Existing producers don't change.

---

## How Deep Tier Workflows Use the Knowledge

### Audit: Drift Detection with Context

When you run audit-skill on a compiled skill, it re-extracts the current source state and compares against the original provenance map. Without Deep tier, you get a structural diff: "export X was added, export Y was removed."

With Deep tier, audit-skill reads the registry, finds the `{skill-name}-extraction` collection, and queries QMD for temporal context on every drifted export:

- When was this export first added?
- Has it been modified recently?
- What is its usage frequency across the codebase?
- How does the current extraction compare to the previously compiled skill content?

The drift report goes from "something changed" to "here's what changed, when it changed, and how significant the change is." T1 structural truth enriched with T2 temporal context.

### Update: Surgical Regeneration

Update-skill re-extracts only changed files (not the entire source), then merges the fresh data with the existing skill while preserving your `[MANUAL]` sections. In Deep tier, it queries the extraction collection for semantic context on each changed export — usage patterns, documentation references, related concepts.

The result: updated instructions that reflect not just what the code *is*, but how it's *used*.

### Portfolio Search: Cross-Skill Intelligence

Brief collections enable a different kind of query entirely. When you brief a new skill, Deep tier can search across all existing brief collections to answer: "Have I already briefed something similar?"

In a large monorepo with dozens of services, this prevents duplicate skill creation. The search isn't matching file names — it's matching scope definitions, target paths, and intent descriptions across your entire skill portfolio.

---

## The Architecture

Three roles. Clean separation. Self-maintaining.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   PRODUCERS                          │
│                                                      │
│  brief-skill ──→ writes brief ──→ indexes            │
│                   {name}-brief collection             │
│                                                      │
│  create-skill ──→ writes artifacts ──→ indexes       │
│                   {name}-extraction collection        │
│                                                      │
├──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┤
│                      │                               │
│              forge-tier.yaml                          │
│              qmd_collections: [...]                   │
│                      │                               │
├──────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────┤
│                   CONSUMERS                          │
│                                                      │
│  audit-skill ──→ reads registry ──→ queries          │
│                   {name}-extraction for drift context │
│                                                      │
│  update-skill ──→ reads registry ──→ queries         │
│                   {name}-extraction for T2 enrichment │
│                                                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                    JANITOR                            │
│                                                      │
│  setup-forge ──→ cross-references QMD state          │
│                   against registry                    │
│                ──→ cleans orphans (with user prompt)  │
│                ──→ removes stale registry entries     │
│                                                      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Producers** (brief-skill, create-skill) create QMD collections from their workflow artifacts and register them. They don't know who consumes the data.

**Consumers** (audit-skill, update-skill) read the registry, discover available collections, and query them. They don't know who produced the data.

**The Janitor** (setup-forge) cross-references live QMD state against the registry on every run. Orphaned collections — present in QMD but not tracked in the registry — are flagged and the user is prompted before removal. Stale registry entries — tracked but missing from QMD — are silently cleaned. The system maintains itself.

---

## Graceful Degradation: Deep Tier Is Never Required

Every Deep tier enhancement follows a strict rule: **QMD failures never block workflows.**

If QMD collection creation fails during create-skill, the seven artifact files are already written. The skill is complete. The collection is a bonus.

If audit-skill can't find an extraction collection in the registry, it logs a notice and continues with T1 structural analysis. The drift report is still useful — it just lacks T2 temporal enrichment.

If setup-forge hygiene encounters a QMD error, it logs and moves on. The forge is still ready.

This isn't defensive coding — it's a design principle. Deep tier adds depth to every workflow without adding fragility. A user who installs QMD gets richer results. A user who uninstalls QMD loses enrichment but keeps everything else. The tier system degrades gracefully because every enhancement is additive, never load-bearing.

---

## The Collection Gate: Knowing When to Stop

Not everything should be a QMD collection. We evaluated three additional candidates and rejected all of them:

**Source clone indexing** — Clone the target repository and index all source files. Rejected: a cloned repo is a snapshot pretending to be current. No clear owner for the refresh lifecycle. The provenance map already records `file:line` for every export. Raw source adds noise, not signal.

**Source-map indexing** — Index the GitHub API tree response as a lightweight directory structure collection. Rejected: no workflow step programmatically searches it. Audit-skill already has file paths from provenance-map.json. A source-map would serve only ad-hoc queries, which don't justify collection overhead.

**Analysis report indexing** — Index the analyze-source decomposition report. Rejected: it's a decision artifact consumed by humans, not a compilation input consumed by workflows. Users can read the file directly.

Each rejection taught us a design principle, which we codified as the **Collection Gate**:

> A new QMD collection type is justified ONLY when a specific workflow step performs a programmatic search against it, the data isn't already accessible through existing artifacts, and QMD search adds value beyond direct file reading.

Human readability alone is not sufficient. The file is already on disk.

This gate keeps the system lean. It prevents speculative indexing — the kind that sounds architecturally elegant but adds maintenance overhead without serving a real consumer. Every collection that passes the gate has a producer that creates it, a consumer that searches it, and a janitor that maintains it.

---

## The Deep Tier Lifecycle

Here's what a full Deep tier workflow looks like end to end:

**Step 1: Setup** — `@Ferris SF`

Ferris detects ast-grep, GitHub CLI, and QMD. Tier: Deep. If previous collections exist, hygiene runs — verifying health, cleaning orphans, removing stale entries. The registry is clean and ready.

**Step 2: Brief** — `@Ferris BS`

You scope a skill through guided discovery. Ferris writes `skill-brief.yaml` to forge-data. Deep tier indexes it into a `{name}-brief` collection and registers it. Portfolio-level search is now available — your next brief can discover existing briefs with similar scope.

**Step 3: Compile** — `@Ferris CS`

Ferris extracts 354 exports from 197 source files. AST-verified signatures, T1 confidence, co-import patterns detected. Seven artifacts written to disk. Deep tier indexes the curated output into a `{name}-extraction` collection and registers it. The extraction data — structured markdown with one section per export — is now searchable.

**Step 4: Audit** — `@Ferris AS`

Six weeks later, the source has evolved. Ferris re-extracts the current state, then queries the extraction collection for temporal context. The drift report shows not just *what* changed, but *when* and *how significantly*. T1 structural truth enriched with T2 temporal intelligence.

**Step 5: Update** — `@Ferris US`

Ferris re-extracts only changed files, queries the extraction collection for semantic context, and merges fresh data with the existing skill — preserving your `[MANUAL]` sections. The updated extraction collection replaces the old one. The registry entry is updated. The knowledge base stays current.

Each step builds on the previous. Each step leaves the system smarter for the next. This is progressive collection building — intelligence that compounds.

---

## What This Means for You

If you're using Quick or Forge tier, nothing changes. Your skills are complete, accurate, and useful at their tier. The progressive capability model means every tier produces legitimate output.

If you install QMD and activate Deep tier, you unlock a layer that Quick and Forge can't provide:

- **Audit context** — Drift reports enriched with temporal intelligence
- **Update precision** — Surgical regeneration informed by usage patterns
- **Portfolio search** — Cross-skill deduplication across your entire project
- **Self-maintaining collections** — Hygiene runs automatically, orphans are flagged, stale entries cleaned
- **Compound intelligence** — Every skill you compile makes every future workflow smarter

The setup is three tools:

```bash
# ast-grep for structural extraction
brew install ast-grep  # or cargo install ast-grep

# GitHub CLI for source access
brew install gh

# QMD for hybrid search
# See https://github.com/tobi/qmd
```

Then:

```
@Ferris SF    # Detects all three tools, sets tier to Deep
@Ferris BS    # Brief — indexed automatically
@Ferris CS    # Compile — indexed automatically
```

Your knowledge base starts building itself.

---

## Try It

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

Setup your forge, brief a skill, compile it. If QMD is available, Deep tier activates automatically. Every compilation enriches the searchable knowledge base. Every audit gets temporal context. Every update gets semantic intelligence.

No configuration. No manual indexing. No maintenance.

Just compile skills. Deep tier handles the rest.

---

**Links:**

- **GitHub:** [armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge)
- **Documentation:** [Architecture deep-dive](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/architecture/)
- **agentskills.io:** [Specification](https://agentskills.io/specification) | [What are skills?](https://agentskills.io/what-are-skills)
- **BMad Method:** [bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD)

---

*Your skills get smarter every time you forge one. That's Deep tier.*

│                                                      │
│  setup-forge ──→ cross-references QMD state          │
│                   against registry                    │
│                ──→ cleans orphans (with user prompt)  │
│                ──→ removes stale registry entries     │
│                                                      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Producers** (brief-skill, create-skill) create QMD collections from their workflow artifacts and register them. Create-skill produces two collections: `{name}-extraction` (curated skill artifacts) and `{name}-temporal` (issues, PRs, changelogs fetched from GitHub via `gh` CLI — cached for 7 days). They don't know who consumes the data.

**Consumers** (create-skill enrichment, audit-skill, update-skill) read the registry, discover available collections, and query them. # Your Agent Skills Get Smarter Every Time You Forge One. Here's How.

*Inside Skill Forge's Deep tier — where compilation builds a searchable knowledge base, automatically.*

---

## The Intelligence Gap

You've compiled an agent skill. It's good — AST-verified signatures, provenance citations, confidence tiers. Your agent stops hallucinating that library's API. Problem solved.

But what happens when you compile your *second* skill? Your *tenth*? When you audit a skill six weeks later and need to understand how the source has drifted?

Each skill compilation produces rich, structured data: 354 verified exports, co-import patterns, confidence ratings, provenance maps. Then that intelligence sits in files on disk — invisible to every subsequent workflow. The next audit-skill run starts from scratch. The next update-skill extraction has no memory of what came before.

The data exists. It's curated, high-confidence, structurally verified. But nothing connects it.

What if every skill you compiled made every future workflow smarter?

---

## Deep Tier: Where Compilation Builds Knowledge

Skill Forge's progressive capability model has three tiers:

- **Quick** — GitHub CLI reads source files. Best-effort extraction.
- **Forge** — ast-grep parses the Abstract Syntax Tree. Structural truth, T1 confidence.
- **Deep** — ast-grep + GitHub CLI + QMD. Everything Forge provides, plus a searchable knowledge layer that grows with every skill you create.

Quick and Forge produce excellent skills. Deep does something fundamentally different: it turns your skill compilation pipeline into a **progressive knowledge base**.

Here's what that means in practice.

---

## Progressive Collection Building

When you compile a skill in Deep tier, the workflow doesn't just write files to disk. After generating all seven artifacts — SKILL.md, context-snippet, metadata, references, provenance map, evidence report, extraction rules — it indexes the curated output into a QMD collection.

```
Artifacts generated.

Deliverables (skills/payment-service/):
- SKILL.md
- context-snippet.md
- metadata.json
- references/ (12 files)

Workspace (forge-data/payment-service/):
- provenance-map.json
- evidence-report.md
- extraction-rules.yaml

QMD collection registered: payment-service-extraction
```

That last line is the Deep tier difference. The structured, confidence-rated extraction data — every function signature, every type definition, every co-import pattern — is now searchable via QMD's hybrid search engine. Keyword matching. Semantic search. LLM-powered re-ranking.

When you brief a skill, the same thing happens:

```
Skill brief written successfully.

File: forge-data/payment-service/skill-brief.yaml
QMD collection registered: payment-service-brief
```

Every workflow step that produces an artifact enriches the searchable knowledge base as a natural side-effect of doing its job. No extra commands. No manual indexing. No configuration.

You compile skills. Deep tier builds knowledge.

---

## Why Curated Data Beats Raw Source

The intuitive approach to search is "index everything." Clone the repo, dump all source files into a search engine, let users query freely. It sounds comprehensive. It's actually noisy.

Consider a real extraction from a knowledge base framework — 197 Python source files containing infrastructure adapters, internal utilities, test helpers, `__init__.py` boilerplate, and the actual public API surface. A raw source index treats all of these equally. Search for "add data" and you get import statements, private helper functions, variable assignments, and — buried somewhere in the results — the actual public function you wanted.

Now consider what the extraction pipeline produces from those same 197 files:

```
Files scanned: ~197 unique Python files
Exports found: ~354 unique entries
  - Functions: ~120 (async functions, static methods)
  - Classes: ~140 (models, adapters, engines, handlers)
  - Types/Enums: ~30
  - Constants/Singletons: ~10
  - Data Models (Pydantic/SQLAlchemy): ~54

Confidence: 354 T1 (source-reading verified), 0 T1-low
```

Each export carries its full signature, file path, line number, parameter types, return type, and confidence tier. The extraction pipeline has already done the hard work of separating signal from noise. When this curated data gets indexed into QMD, every search returns *the right thing* — because there's no wrong thing in the collection.

Search "add data" against the extraction collection and you get:

```
async def add(data, dataset_name, ...) — ingest data
[AST:cognee/__init__.py:L42] Confidence: T1
```

One result. The right result. With provenance.

This is the core insight: **index the output of intelligence, not the input to it**.

---

## The Registry: Connective Tissue

Collections don't float in the void. Every QMD collection created by a workflow is registered in `forge-tier.yaml` — the same file that tracks tool availability and capability tier:

```yaml
tools:
  ast_grep: true
  gh_cli: true
  qmd: true

tier: Deep
tier_detected_at: "2026-03-14T10:30:00Z"

qmd_collections:
  - name: "payment-service-extraction"
    type: "extraction"
    source_workflow: "create-skill"
    skill_name: "payment-service"
    created_at: "2026-03-14"
  - name: "payment-service-brief"
    type: "brief"
    source_workflow: "brief-skill"
    skill_name: "payment-service"
    created_at: "2026-03-14"
  - name: "payment-service-temporal"
    type: "temporal"
    source_workflow: "create-skill"
    skill_name: "payment-service"
    created_at: "2026-03-14"
```

Six lines per collection, three collection types. The registry serves as the single source of truth for what's been indexed. Downstream workflows don't guess — they read the registry, find collections by skill name and type, and query precisely what they need.

This decoupling is deliberate. The workflow that *creates* a collection (create-skill) has no knowledge of the workflows that *consume* it (audit-skill, update-skill). They communicate through the registry. Add a new producer? Consumers discover it automatically. Add a new consumer? Existing producers don't change.

---

## How Deep Tier Workflows Use the Knowledge

### Audit: Drift Detection with Context

When you run audit-skill on a compiled skill, it re-extracts the current source state and compares against the original provenance map. Without Deep tier, you get a structural diff: "export X was added, export Y was removed."

With Deep tier, audit-skill reads the registry, finds the `{skill-name}-extraction` collection, and queries QMD for temporal context on every drifted export:

- When was this export first added?
- Has it been modified recently?
- What is its usage frequency across the codebase?
- How does the current extraction compare to the previously compiled skill content?

The drift report goes from "something changed" to "here's what changed, when it changed, and how significant the change is." T1 structural truth enriched with T2 temporal context.

### Update: Surgical Regeneration

Update-skill re-extracts only changed files (not the entire source), then merges the fresh data with the existing skill while preserving your `[MANUAL]` sections. In Deep tier, it queries the extraction collection for semantic context on each changed export — usage patterns, documentation references, related concepts.

The result: updated instructions that reflect not just what the code *is*, but how it's *used*.

### Portfolio Search: Cross-Skill Intelligence

Brief collections enable a different kind of query entirely. When you brief a new skill, Deep tier can search across all existing brief collections to answer: "Have I already briefed something similar?"

In a large monorepo with dozens of services, this prevents duplicate skill creation. The search isn't matching file names — it's matching scope definitions, target paths, and intent descriptions across your entire skill portfolio.

---

## The Architecture

Three roles. Clean separation. Self-maintaining.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   PRODUCERS                          │
│                                                      │
│  brief-skill ──→ writes brief ──→ indexes            │
│                   {name}-brief collection             │
│                                                      │
│  create-skill ──→ writes artifacts ──→ indexes       │
│                   {name}-extraction collection        │
│                                                      │
├──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┤
│                      │                               │
│              forge-tier.yaml                          │
│              qmd_collections: [...]                   │
│                      │                               │
├──────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────┤
│                   CONSUMERS                          │
│                                                      │
│  audit-skill ──→ reads registry ──→ queries          │
│                   {name}-extraction for drift context │
│                                                      │
│  update-skill ──→ reads registry ──→ queries         │
│                   {name}-extraction for T2 enrichment │
│                                                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                    JANITOR                            │
│                                                      │
│  setup-forge ──→ cross-references QMD state          │
│                   against registry                    │
│                ──→ cleans orphans (with user prompt)  │
│                ──→ removes stale registry entries     │
│                                                      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Producers** (brief-skill, create-skill) create QMD collections from their workflow artifacts and register them. They don't know who consumes the data.

**Consumers** (audit-skill, update-skill) read the registry, discover available collections, and query them. They don't know who produced the data.

**The Janitor** (setup-forge) cross-references live QMD state against the registry on every run. Orphaned collections — present in QMD but not tracked in the registry — are flagged and the user is prompted before removal. Stale registry entries — tracked but missing from QMD — are silently cleaned. The system maintains itself.

---

## Graceful Degradation: Deep Tier Is Never Required

Every Deep tier enhancement follows a strict rule: **QMD failures never block workflows.**

If QMD collection creation fails during create-skill, the seven artifact files are already written. The skill is complete. The collection is a bonus.

If audit-skill can't find an extraction collection in the registry, it logs a notice and continues with T1 structural analysis. The drift report is still useful — it just lacks T2 temporal enrichment.

If setup-forge hygiene encounters a QMD error, it logs and moves on. The forge is still ready.

This isn't defensive coding — it's a design principle. Deep tier adds depth to every workflow without adding fragility. A user who installs QMD gets richer results. A user who uninstalls QMD loses enrichment but keeps everything else. The tier system degrades gracefully because every enhancement is additive, never load-bearing.

---

## The Collection Gate: Knowing When to Stop

Not everything should be a QMD collection. We evaluated three additional candidates and rejected all of them:

**Source clone indexing** — Clone the target repository and index all source files. Rejected: a cloned repo is a snapshot pretending to be current. No clear owner for the refresh lifecycle. The provenance map already records `file:line` for every export. Raw source adds noise, not signal.

**Source-map indexing** — Index the GitHub API tree response as a lightweight directory structure collection. Rejected: no workflow step programmatically searches it. Audit-skill already has file paths from provenance-map.json. A source-map would serve only ad-hoc queries, which don't justify collection overhead.

**Analysis report indexing** — Index the analyze-source decomposition report. Rejected: it's a decision artifact consumed by humans, not a compilation input consumed by workflows. Users can read the file directly.

Each rejection taught us a design principle, which we codified as the **Collection Gate**:

> A new QMD collection type is justified ONLY when a specific workflow step performs a programmatic search against it, the data isn't already accessible through existing artifacts, and QMD search adds value beyond direct file reading.

Human readability alone is not sufficient. The file is already on disk.

This gate keeps the system lean. It prevents speculative indexing — the kind that sounds architecturally elegant but adds maintenance overhead without serving a real consumer. Every collection that passes the gate has a producer that creates it, a consumer that searches it, and a janitor that maintains it.

---

## The Deep Tier Lifecycle

Here's what a full Deep tier workflow looks like end to end:

**Step 1: Setup** — `@Ferris SF`

Ferris detects ast-grep, GitHub CLI, and QMD. Tier: Deep. If previous collections exist, hygiene runs — verifying health, cleaning orphans, removing stale entries. The registry is clean and ready.

**Step 2: Brief** — `@Ferris BS`

You scope a skill through guided discovery. Ferris writes `skill-brief.yaml` to forge-data. Deep tier indexes it into a `{name}-brief` collection and registers it. Portfolio-level search is now available — your next brief can discover existing briefs with similar scope.

**Step 3: Compile** — `@Ferris CS`

Ferris extracts 354 exports from 197 source files. AST-verified signatures, T1 confidence, co-import patterns detected. Seven artifacts written to disk. Deep tier indexes the curated output into a `{name}-extraction` collection and registers it. The extraction data — structured markdown with one section per export — is now searchable.

**Step 4: Audit** — `@Ferris AS`

Six weeks later, the source has evolved. Ferris re-extracts the current state, then queries the extraction collection for temporal context. The drift report shows not just *what* changed, but *when* and *how significantly*. T1 structural truth enriched with T2 temporal intelligence.

**Step 5: Update** — `@Ferris US`

Ferris re-extracts only changed files, queries the extraction collection for semantic context, and merges fresh data with the existing skill — preserving your `[MANUAL]` sections. The updated extraction collection replaces the old one. The registry entry is updated. The knowledge base stays current.

Each step builds on the previous. Each step leaves the system smarter for the next. This is progressive collection building — intelligence that compounds.

---

## What This Means for You

If you're using Quick or Forge tier, nothing changes. Your skills are complete, accurate, and useful at their tier. The progressive capability model means every tier produces legitimate output.

If you install QMD and activate Deep tier, you unlock a layer that Quick and Forge can't provide:

- **Audit context** — Drift reports enriched with temporal intelligence
- **Update precision** — Surgical regeneration informed by usage patterns
- **Portfolio search** — Cross-skill deduplication across your entire project
- **Self-maintaining collections** — Hygiene runs automatically, orphans are flagged, stale entries cleaned
- **Compound intelligence** — Every skill you compile makes every future workflow smarter

The setup is three tools:

```bash
# ast-grep for structural extraction
brew install ast-grep  # or cargo install ast-grep

# GitHub CLI for source access
brew install gh

# QMD for hybrid search
# See https://github.com/tobi/qmd
```

Then:

```
@Ferris SF    # Detects all three tools, sets tier to Deep
@Ferris BS    # Brief — indexed automatically
@Ferris CS    # Compile — indexed automatically
```

Your knowledge base starts building itself.

---

## Try It

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

Setup your forge, brief a skill, compile it. If QMD is available, Deep tier activates automatically. Every compilation enriches the searchable knowledge base. Every audit gets temporal context. Every update gets semantic intelligence.

No configuration. No manual indexing. No maintenance.

Just compile skills. Deep tier handles the rest.

---

**Links:**

- **GitHub:** [armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge)
- **Documentation:** [Architecture deep-dive](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/architecture/)
- **agentskills.io:** [Specification](https://agentskills.io/specification) | [What are skills?](https://agentskills.io/what-are-skills)
- **BMad Method:** [bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD)

---

*Your skills get smarter every time you forge one. That's Deep tier.*

QMD collection registered: payment-service-brief
```

Every workflow step that produces an artifact enriches the searchable knowledge base as a natural side-effect of doing its job. No extra commands. No manual indexing. No configuration.

You compile skills. Deep tier builds knowledge.

---

## Why Curated Data Beats Raw Source

The intuitive approach to search is "index everything." Clone the repo, dump all source files into a search engine, let users query freely. It sounds comprehensive. It's actually noisy.

Consider a real extraction from a knowledge base framework — 197 Python source files containing infrastructure adapters, internal utilities, test helpers, `__init__.py` boilerplate, and the actual public API surface. A raw source index treats all of these equally. Search for "add data" and you get import statements, private helper functions, variable assignments, and — buried somewhere in the results — the actual public function you wanted.

Now consider what the extraction pipeline produces from those same 197 files:

```
Files scanned: ~197 unique Python files
Exports found: ~354 unique entries
  - Functions: ~120 (async functions, static methods)
  - Classes: ~140 (models, adapters, engines, handlers)
  - Types/Enums: ~30
  - Constants/Singletons: ~10
  - Data Models (Pydantic/SQLAlchemy): ~54

Confidence: 354 T1 (source-reading verified), 0 T1-low
```

Each export carries its full signature, file path, line number, parameter types, return type, and confidence tier. The extraction pipeline has already done the hard work of separating signal from noise. When this curated data gets indexed into QMD, every search returns *the right thing* — because there's no wrong thing in the collection.

Search "add data" against the extraction collection and you get:

```
async def add(data, dataset_name, ...) — ingest data
[AST:cognee/__init__.py:L42] Confidence: T1
```

One result. The right result. With provenance.

This is the core insight: **index the output of intelligence, not the input to it**.

---

## The Registry: Connective Tissue

Collections don't float in the void. Every QMD collection created by a workflow is registered in `forge-tier.yaml` — the same file that tracks tool availability and capability tier:

```yaml
tools:
  ast_grep: true
  gh_cli: true
  qmd: true

tier: Deep
tier_detected_at: "2026-03-14T10:30:00Z"

qmd_collections:
  - name: "payment-service-extraction"
    type: "extraction"
    source_workflow: "create-skill"
    skill_name: "payment-service"
    created_at: "2026-03-14"
  - name: "payment-service-brief"
    type: "brief"
    source_workflow: "brief-skill"
    skill_name: "payment-service"
    created_at: "2026-03-14"
  - name: "payment-service-temporal"
    type: "temporal"
    source_workflow: "create-skill"
    skill_name: "payment-service"
    created_at: "2026-03-14"
```

Six lines per collection, three collection types. The registry serves as the single source of truth for what's been indexed. Downstream workflows don't guess — they read the registry, find collections by skill name and type, and query precisely what they need.

This decoupling is deliberate. The workflow that *creates* a collection (create-skill) has no knowledge of the workflows that *consume* it (audit-skill, update-skill). They communicate through the registry. Add a new producer? Consumers discover it automatically. Add a new consumer? Existing producers don't change.

---

## How Deep Tier Workflows Use the Knowledge

### Audit: Drift Detection with Context

When you run audit-skill on a compiled skill, it re-extracts the current source state and compares against the original provenance map. Without Deep tier, you get a structural diff: "export X was added, export Y was removed."

With Deep tier, audit-skill reads the registry, finds the `{skill-name}-extraction` collection, and queries QMD for temporal context on every drifted export:

- When was this export first added?
- Has it been modified recently?
- What is its usage frequency across the codebase?
- How does the current extraction compare to the previously compiled skill content?

The drift report goes from "something changed" to "here's what changed, when it changed, and how significant the change is." T1 structural truth enriched with T2 temporal context.

### Update: Surgical Regeneration

Update-skill re-extracts only changed files (not the entire source), then merges the fresh data with the existing skill while preserving your `[MANUAL]` sections. In Deep tier, it queries the extraction collection for semantic context on each changed export — usage patterns, documentation references, related concepts.

The result: updated instructions that reflect not just what the code *is*, but how it's *used*.

### Portfolio Search: Cross-Skill Intelligence

Brief collections enable a different kind of query entirely. When you brief a new skill, Deep tier can search across all existing brief collections to answer: "Have I already briefed something similar?"

In a large monorepo with dozens of services, this prevents duplicate skill creation. The search isn't matching file names — it's matching scope definitions, target paths, and intent descriptions across your entire skill portfolio.

---

## The Architecture

Three roles. Clean separation. Self-maintaining.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   PRODUCERS                          │
│                                                      │
│  brief-skill ──→ writes brief ──→ indexes            │
│                   {name}-brief collection             │
│                                                      │
│  create-skill ──→ writes artifacts ──→ indexes       │
│                   {name}-extraction collection        │
│                                                      │
│  create-skill ──→ fetches issues/PRs/releases        │
│                   ──→ indexes {name}-temporal         │
│                   (GitHub repos only, 7-day cache)    │
│                                                      │
├──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┤
│                      │                               │
│              forge-tier.yaml                          │
│              qmd_collections: [...]                   │
│                      │                               │
├──────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────┤
│                   CONSUMERS                          │
│                                                      │
│  create-skill ──→ reads registry ──→ queries         │
│                   {name}-temporal for T2 enrichment   │
│                                                      │
│  audit-skill ──→ reads registry ──→ queries          │
│                   {name}-extraction for drift context │
│                                                      │
│  update-skill ──→ reads registry ──→ queries         │
│                   {name}-extraction for T2 enrichment │
│                                                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                    JANITOR                            │
│                                                      │
│  setup-forge ──→ cross-references QMD state          │
│                   against registry                    │
│                ──→ cleans orphans (with user prompt)  │
│                ──→ removes stale registry entries     │
│                                                      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Producers** (brief-skill, create-skill) create QMD collections from their workflow artifacts and register them. Create-skill produces two collections: `{name}-extraction` (curated skill artifacts) and `{name}-temporal` (issues, PRs, changelogs fetched from GitHub via `gh` CLI — cached for 7 days). They don't know who consumes the data.

**Consumers** (create-skill enrichment, audit-skill, update-skill) read the registry, discover available collections, and query them. Create-skill is both a producer and a consumer — it fetches temporal context in step 3b, then searches it in step 4 during enrichment. They don't know who produced the data.

**The Janitor** (setup-forge) cross-references live QMD state against the registry on every run. Orphaned collections — present in QMD but not tracked in the registry — are flagged and the user is prompted before removal. Stale registry entries — tracked but missing from QMD — are silently cleaned. The system maintains itself.

---

## Graceful Degradation: Deep Tier Is Never Required

Every Deep tier enhancement follows a strict rule: **QMD failures never block workflows.**

If QMD collection creation fails during create-skill, the seven artifact files are already written. The skill is complete. The collection is a bonus.

If audit-skill can't find an extraction collection in the registry, it logs a notice and continues with T1 structural analysis. The drift report is still useful — it just lacks T2 temporal enrichment.

If setup-forge hygiene encounters a QMD error, it logs and moves on. The forge is still ready.

This isn't defensive coding — it's a design principle. Deep tier adds depth to every workflow without adding fragility. A user who installs QMD gets richer results. A user who uninstalls QMD loses enrichment but keeps everything else. The tier system degrades gracefully because every enhancement is additive, never load-bearing.

---

## The Collection Gate: Knowing When to Stop

Not everything should be a QMD collection. We evaluated three additional candidates and rejected all of them:

**Source clone indexing** — Clone the target repository and index all source files. Rejected: a cloned repo is a snapshot pretending to be current. No clear owner for the refresh lifecycle. The provenance map already records `file:line` for every export. Raw source adds noise, not signal.

**Source-map indexing** — Index the GitHub API tree response as a lightweight directory structure collection. Rejected: no workflow step programmatically searches it. Audit-skill already has file paths from provenance-map.json. A source-map would serve only ad-hoc queries, which don't justify collection overhead.

**Analysis report indexing** — Index the analyze-source decomposition report. Rejected: it's a decision artifact consumed by humans, not a compilation input consumed by workflows. Users can read the file directly.

Each rejection taught us a design principle, which we codified as the **Collection Gate**:

> A new QMD collection type is justified ONLY when a specific workflow step performs a programmatic search against it, the data isn't already accessible through existing artifacts, and QMD search adds value beyond direct file reading.

Human readability alone is not sufficient. The file is already on disk.

This gate keeps the system lean. It prevents speculative indexing — the kind that sounds architecturally elegant but adds maintenance overhead without serving a real consumer. Every collection that passes the gate has a producer that creates it, a consumer that searches it, and a janitor that maintains it.

---

## The Deep Tier Lifecycle

Here's what a full Deep tier workflow looks like end to end:

**Step 1: Setup** — `@Ferris SF`

Ferris detects ast-grep, GitHub CLI, and QMD. Tier: Deep. If previous collections exist, hygiene runs — verifying health, cleaning orphans, removing stale entries. The registry is clean and ready.

**Step 2: Brief** — `@Ferris BS`

You scope a skill through guided discovery. Ferris writes `skill-brief.yaml` to forge-data. Deep tier indexes it into a `{name}-brief` collection and registers it. Portfolio-level search is now available — your next brief can discover existing briefs with similar scope.

**Step 3: Compile** — `@Ferris CS`

Ferris extracts 354 exports from 197 source files. AST-verified signatures, T1 confidence, co-import patterns detected. Seven artifacts written to disk. Deep tier indexes the curated output into a `{name}-extraction` collection and registers it. The extraction data — structured markdown with one section per export — is now searchable.

**Step 4: Audit** — `@Ferris AS`

Six weeks later, the source has evolved. Ferris re-extracts the current state, then queries the extraction collection for temporal context. The drift report shows not just *what* changed, but *when* and *how significantly*. T1 structural truth enriched with T2 temporal intelligence.

**Step 5: Update** — `@Ferris US`

Ferris re-extracts only changed files, queries the extraction collection for semantic context, and merges fresh data with the existing skill — preserving your `[MANUAL]` sections. The updated extraction collection replaces the old one. The registry entry is updated. The knowledge base stays current.

Each step builds on the previous. Each step leaves the system smarter for the next. This is progressive collection building — intelligence that compounds.

---

## What This Means for You

If you're using Quick or Forge tier, nothing changes. Your skills are complete, accurate, and useful at their tier. The progressive capability model means every tier produces legitimate output.

If you install QMD and activate Deep tier, you unlock a layer that Quick and Forge can't provide:

- **Audit context** — Drift reports enriched with temporal intelligence
- **Update precision** — Surgical regeneration informed by usage patterns
- **Portfolio search** — Cross-skill deduplication across your entire project
- **Self-maintaining collections** — Hygiene runs automatically, orphans are flagged, stale entries cleaned
- **Compound intelligence** — Every skill you compile makes every future workflow smarter

The setup is three tools:

```bash
# ast-grep for structural extraction
brew install ast-grep  # or cargo install ast-grep

# GitHub CLI for source access
brew install gh

# QMD for hybrid search
# See https://github.com/tobi/qmd
```

Then:

```
@Ferris SF    # Detects all three tools, sets tier to Deep
@Ferris BS    # Brief — indexed automatically
@Ferris CS    # Compile — indexed automatically
```

Your knowledge base starts building itself.

---

## Try It

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

Setup your forge, brief a skill, compile it. If QMD is available, Deep tier activates automatically. Every compilation enriches the searchable knowledge base. Every audit gets temporal context. Every update gets semantic intelligence.

No configuration. No manual indexing. No maintenance.

Just compile skills. Deep tier handles the rest.

---

# Your Agent Skills Get Smarter Every Time You Forge One. Here's How.

*Inside Skill Forge's Deep tier — where compilation builds a searchable knowledge base, automatically.*

---

## The Intelligence Gap

You've compiled an agent skill. It's good — AST-verified signatures, provenance citations, confidence tiers. Your agent stops hallucinating that library's API. Problem solved.

But what happens when you compile your *second* skill? Your *tenth*? When you audit a skill six weeks later and need to understand how the source has drifted?

Each skill compilation produces rich, structured data: 354 verified exports, co-import patterns, confidence ratings, provenance maps. Then that intelligence sits in files on disk — invisible to every subsequent workflow. The next audit-skill run starts from scratch. The next update-skill extraction has no memory of what came before.

The data exists. It's curated, high-confidence, structurally verified. But nothing connects it.

What if every skill you compiled made every future workflow smarter?

---

## Deep Tier: Where Compilation Builds Knowledge

Skill Forge's progressive capability model has three tiers:

- **Quick** — GitHub CLI reads source files. Best-effort extraction.
- **Forge** — ast-grep parses the Abstract Syntax Tree. Structural truth, T1 confidence.
- **Deep** — ast-grep + GitHub CLI + QMD. Everything Forge provides, plus a searchable knowledge layer that grows with every skill you create.

Quick and Forge produce excellent skills. Deep does something fundamentally different: it turns your skill compilation pipeline into a **progressive knowledge base**.

Here's what that means in practice.

---

## Progressive Collection Building

When you compile a skill in Deep tier, the workflow doesn't just write files to disk. After generating all seven artifacts — SKILL.md, context-snippet, metadata, references, provenance map, evidence report, extraction rules — it indexes the curated output into a QMD collection.

```
Artifacts generated.

Deliverables (skills/payment-service/):
- SKILL.md
- context-snippet.md
- metadata.json
- references/ (12 files)

Workspace (forge-data/payment-service/):
- provenance-map.json
- evidence-report.md
- extraction-rules.yaml

QMD collection registered: payment-service-extraction
```

That last line is the Deep tier difference. The structured, confidence-rated extraction data — every function signature, every type definition, every co-import pattern — is now searchable via QMD's hybrid search engine. Keyword matching. Semantic search. LLM-powered re-ranking.

When you brief a skill, the same thing happens:

```
Skill brief written successfully.

File: forge-data/payment-service/skill-brief.yaml
QMD collection registered: payment-service-brief
```

Every workflow step that produces an artifact enriches the searchable knowledge base as a natural side-effect of doing its job. No extra commands. No manual indexing. No configuration.

You compile skills. Deep tier builds knowledge.

Here's what a full Deep tier workflow looks like end to end:

**Step 1: Setup** — `@Ferris SF`

Ferris detects ast-grep, GitHub CLI, and QMD. Tier: Deep. If previous collections exist, hygiene runs — verifying health, cleaning orphans, removing stale entries. The registry is clean and ready.

**Step 2: Brief** — `@Ferris BS`

You scope a skill through guided discovery. Ferris writes `skill-brief.yaml` to forge-data. Deep tier indexes it into a `{name}-brief` collection and registers it. Portfolio-level search is now available — your next brief can discover existing briefs with similar scope.

**Step 3: Compile** — `@Ferris CS`

Ferris extracts 354 exports from 197 source files. AST-verified signatures, T1 confidence, co-import patterns detected. But before enrichment, Deep tier fetches temporal context — the last 100 issues, 100 merged PRs, 10 releases, and any CHANGELOG.md from the source repository via `gh` CLI. This gets indexed into a `{name}-temporal` collection and cached for 7 days (so update-skill reruns don't re-fetch).

Then enrichment searches the temporal collection for each major public API function — finding related issues, deprecation warnings, and version history. T2 annotations layer on top of T1 extraction.

Seven artifacts written to disk. Deep tier indexes the curated output into a `{name}-extraction` collection and registers it. The extraction data — structured markdown with one section per export — is now searchable.

**Step 4: Audit** — `@Ferris AS`

Six weeks later, the source has evolved. Ferris re-extracts the current state, then queries the extraction collection for temporal context. The drift report shows not just *what* changed, but *when* and *how significantly*. T1 structural truth enriched with T2 temporal intelligence.

**Step 5: Update** — `@Ferris US`

Ferris re-extracts only changed files, queries the extraction collection for semantic context, and merges fresh data with the existing skill — preserving your `[MANUAL]` sections. The updated extraction collection replaces the old one. The registry entry is updated. The knowledge base stays current.

Each step builds on the previous. Each step leaves the system smarter for the next. This is progressive collection building — intelligence that compounds.

---

## What This Means for You

If you're using Quick or Forge tier, nothing changes. Your skills are complete, accurate, and useful at their tier. The progressive capability model means every tier produces legitimate output.

If you install QMD and activate Deep tier, you unlock a layer that Quick and Forge can't provide:

- **Audit context** — Drift reports enriched with temporal intelligence
- **Update precision** — Surgical regeneration informed by usage patterns
- **Portfolio search** — Cross-skill deduplication across your entire project
- **Self-maintaining collections** — Hygiene runs automatically, orphans are flagged, stale entries cleaned
- **Compound intelligence** — Every skill you compile makes every future workflow smarter

The setup is three tools:

```bash
# ast-grep for structural extraction
brew install ast-grep  # or cargo install ast-grep

# GitHub CLI for source access
brew install gh

# QMD for hybrid search
# See https://github.com/tobi/qmd
```

Then:

```
@Ferris SF    # Detects all three tools, sets tier to Deep
@Ferris BS    # Brief — indexed automatically
@Ferris CS    # Compile — indexed automatically
```

Your knowledge base starts building itself.

---

## Try It

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

Setup your forge, brief a skill, compile it. If QMD is available, Deep tier activates automatically. Every compilation enriches the searchable knowledge base. Every audit gets temporal context. Every update gets semantic intelligence.

No configuration. No manual indexing. No maintenance.

Just compile skills. Deep tier handles the rest.

---

**Links:**

- **GitHub:** [armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge)
- **Documentation:** [Architecture deep-dive](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/architecture/)
- **agentskills.io:** [Specification](https://agentskills.io/specification) | [What are skills?](https://agentskills.io/what-are-skills)
- **BMad Method:** [bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD)

---

*Your skills get smarter every time you forge one. That's Deep tier.*

lines: ## The Collection Gate: Knowing When to Stop
-Not everything should be a QMD collection. We evaluated three additional candidates and rejected all of them:
+Not everything should be a QMD collection. We accepted one additional candidate and rejected three others.

+**Temporal context indexing** — Fetch issues, PRs, changelogs, and release notes from the source repository and index them as a `{name}-temporal` collection. Accepted: the create-skill enrichment step (step 4) programmatically searches it per public API function to add T2 annotations. The data isn't in extraction artifacts (which contain current-state signatures, not historical context). QMD search adds value — keyword matching finds "deprecated" in issue titles, semantic search finds related discussions even when vocabulary differs. This collection passed the gate on all three criteria.

**Source clone indexing** — Clone the target repository and index all source files. Rejected: a cloned repo is a snapshot pretending to be current. No clear owner for the refresh lifecycle. The provenance map already records `file:line` for every export. Raw source adds noise, not signal.

**Source-map indexing** — Index the GitHub API tree response as a lightweight directory structure collection. Rejected: no workflow step programmatically searches it. Audit-skill already has file paths from provenance-map.json. A source-map would serve only ad-hoc queries, which don't justify collection overhead.

# Your AI Agent is Hallucinating Your APIs. Here's How to Fix It Permanently.

*How I built an evidence-based agent skills compiler in 10 days — and why every instruction traces to code.*

---

## The Problem No One Talks About

Your AI agent just invented a function that doesn't exist. Again.

You're building a payment integration. You ask your agent to call the auth library's `refreshToken` method. It confidently writes `refreshToken(userId, { force: true })`. The code looks right. The types look plausible. You ship it. It explodes at runtime because the actual signature is `refreshToken(sessionId: string): Promise<AuthToken>` — no options parameter, and it takes a session ID, not a user ID.

This isn't a rare edge case. It's Tuesday.

AI agents hallucinate APIs constantly. They guess function signatures from training data patterns. They invent parameters that sound reasonable. They confidently produce code that compiles, passes type checks, and fails in production — because the agent never actually read your source code.

The standard fixes don't work at scale:

| Approach | Strength | Fatal Flaw |
|----------|----------|------------|
| `npx skills init` | Format compliant | Empty shell. 0% intelligence. |
| LLM Summarization | High semantic context | Hallucination. Guesses parameters. |
| RAG / Context stuffing | Good retrieval | Fragmented. Finds snippets, can't synthesize. |
| Manual skill authoring | High initial quality | Drift. Doesn't scale past 2 libraries. |
| Copilot/Cursor built-in | Convenient | Generic. Doesn't know YOUR patterns. |

Every approach either hallucinates, doesn't scale, or drifts out of date the moment the source changes.

What if there was a way to make every instruction trace directly to your actual code?

---

## What If Every Instruction Traced to Code?

Here's what a Skill Forge output looks like:

```
Extracted: getToken(userId: string, options?: TokenOptions): Promise<AuthToken>
[AST:src/auth/index.ts:L42]. Confidence: T1.
```

That `[AST:src/auth/index.ts:L42]` tag isn't decoration. It means the tool mechanically parsed your source code's Abstract Syntax Tree, found this exact function signature at line 42 of that file, and bound the instruction to it. This isn't a summary. This isn't a guess. The compiler *read your code* and produced a verifiable citation.

This is what Skill Forge (SKF) does. It's an automated skill compiler for the AI agent ecosystem. It transforms source code into [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io)-compliant, version-pinned, provenance-backed agent skills. Every function signature, every type definition, every usage pattern — mechanically extracted and structurally verified.

The output isn't a document. It's evidence.

---

## 47 Seconds to Your First Skill

You don't need to understand the architecture to use SKF. You need three commands.

Install the module:

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

Set up your environment:

```
@Ferris SF
```

Generate a skill for any library:

```
@Ferris QS https://github.com/topoteretes/cognee
```

Ferris — SKF's sole agent — resolves the repository, reads the source, extracts the public API, validates against the agentskills.io spec, and writes a complete skill to your `skills/` directory. Forty-seven seconds. Your agent stops hallucinating that library's API. Done.

That's Quick mode — no AST tooling needed, just the GitHub CLI. But SKF is designed to grow with you.

---

## Quick, Forge, Deep — Meet Developers Where They Are

SKF uses a progressive capability model. Each tier is the previous tier plus one tool. You never lose capability by adding tools. You only gain precision.

**Quick mode** — just `gh` (GitHub CLI). Reads source files directly. Best-effort extraction. Skills marked as `community` authority. Good enough for most cases. Available right now, zero setup beyond the GitHub CLI you probably already have.

**Forge mode** — add `ast-grep`. Now SKF doesn't just read your code — it *parses the Abstract Syntax Tree*. Function signatures are structurally extracted, not pattern-matched from text. Co-import patterns between libraries are detected automatically. Skills earn `official` authority status. Confidence jumps from best-effort to T1 — AST-verified, immutable for that version.

**Deep mode** — add `QMD` (a local hybrid search engine). As skills are compiled, SKF indexes curated extraction artifacts — structured, confidence-rated export data — into QMD collections. Then it uses keyword search, vector semantic search, and LLM-powered re-ranking to surface context no other tool can find. Why was that parameter deprecated? What breaking change is coming in the next version? Deep mode answers these questions by searching across your indexed skill artifacts and briefs.

The tiers aren't modes you choose. Setup detects your installed tools and sets your tier automatically:

```
Forge initialized. Tools: gh, ast-grep, QMD. Tier: Deep. Ready.
```

Install a tool later, and your tier upgrades silently. Uninstall one, and it degrades gracefully with clear messaging about what you've lost.

---

## What You Actually Get

Every generated skill produces a self-contained directory:

```
skills/payment-service/
  SKILL.md              -- Active skill (loaded when triggered)
  context-snippet.md    -- Passive context (compressed, always-on)
  metadata.json         -- Machine-readable provenance
  references/           -- Progressive disclosure
    getToken.md
    refreshToken.md
    createSession.md
```

### The Skill File

`SKILL.md` follows the [agentskills.io specification](https://agentskills.io/specification). Frontmatter pins the version:

```yaml
---
name: payment-service
version: 2.1.0
description: Payment processing API skill — 23 verified functions
author: org/payment-team
---
```

The body contains instructions with provenance citations. Every claim traces to source. No instruction exists without evidence.

### The Birth Certificate

`metadata.json` is a machine-readable provenance record:

```json
{
  "source_repo": "github.com/org/payment-service",
  "source_commit": "a1b2c3d",
  "forge_tier": "forge",
  "stats": {
    "exports_documented": 23,
    "exports_total": 23,
    "coverage": 1.0,
    "confidence_t1": 20,
    "confidence_t2": 3
  }
}
```

You know exactly what commit this skill was generated from, what tier produced it, and how many exports have T1 (AST-verified) vs T2 (knowledge-enriched) confidence. Full transparency.

### The Dual-Output Strategy

Here's something most people miss about agent skills: [Vercel's research](https://github.com/vercel-labs/skills) found that passive context (injected into AGENTS.md or CLAUDE.md) achieves a 100% task pass rate, compared to 53% for active skills alone.

So every skill generates both:

1. **SKILL.md** — the full active skill loaded on trigger
2. **context-snippet.md** — a compressed index automatically injected into your CLAUDE.md

When you run `@Ferris EX` (export), SKF writes a managed section between `<!-- SKF:BEGIN -->` and `<!-- SKF:END -->` markers in your CLAUDE.md. Two lines per skill, ~30 tokens each. Your agent always has a lightweight index of what skills exist and what they cover — without loading the full skill until it's needed.

Developer controls placement. Ferris controls content. The best of both worlds.

---

## How I Built This in 10 Days

This is the part of the article where I'm supposed to say "I built this over a weekend." I didn't. I built it over 10 days — but not the way you'd expect.

SKF was designed and built collaboratively with AI agents using the [BMAD method](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD), an agent-orchestrated development framework. The entire journey — every architectural decision, every design trade-off, every workflow specification — is recorded in a persistent memory system with 71 traceable observations across 39 sessions.

Here's how it actually happened:

**Day 1 (Feb 25) — The spark.** The problem was clear: AI agents hallucinate APIs, and no existing tool fixes it at scale. I started a party mode session — a multi-agent brainstorming discussion — with the full BMAD agent team. The business analyst identified the competitive gap. The architect proposed AST-backed provenance. The product manager asked "why?" until we found the core value: structural truth over semantic guessing.

That afternoon, the team refined SKF's identity through five creative analysis methods. By evening, we had the module brief: 1 agent, 10 workflows, 4 MCP tools, 8 user scenarios, and a progressive capability model.

**Day 1, continued — Ferris is born.** The agent specification defined Ferris as "Skill Architect & Integrity Guardian" with four workflow-driven modes (Architect, Surgeon, Audit, Delivery) and a forge/metallurgy personality that appears at transitions and vanishes during work. The communication style: structured reports with AST citations during execution, warm forge language at transitions, quiet craftsman's pride on completion.

**Days 2-3 (Feb 26-27) — Workflow design marathon.** Each of the 10 workflows went through a structured design process: discovery questions, classification, requirements gathering, tool configuration, plan review, approval, and build. The create-skill workflow alone produced an 8-step compilation pipeline with two strategic user confirmation gates, tier-dependent behavior, and 7 output files across two directory trees.

The quick-skill workflow passed critical path validation with zero violations — every file reference verified, no hardcoded paths, proper module isolation confirmed.

**Days 4-7 (Feb 28 - Mar 2) — Implementation.** The full module was implemented: Ferris agent, 10 end-to-end workflows, 8 cross-cutting knowledge fragments (zero-hallucination enforcement, confidence tiers, provenance tracking, guardrails, validation loops, observability, versioning, lifecycle governance), sidecar state management with just-in-time knowledge loading, help catalog, and documentation.

**Days 8-10 (Mar 3-6) — Ship.** Documentation site built with Astro/Starlight. Custom forge-themed logo. CLI installer (`npx bmad-module-skill-forge install`). npm package published. GitHub Pages deployed.

71 decisions. 10 workflows. 1 agent. Zero hallucination tolerance. Every decision traceable in the memory system.

The meta-lesson: I used AI agents to build a tool that makes AI agents smarter. The BMAD method's multi-agent collaboration wasn't just efficient — it caught design issues I would have missed solo. The QA agent questioned edge cases in the confidence tier system. The architect pushed back on multi-agent designs that would fragment context. The tech writer enforced documentation standards on every workflow specification.

---

## Stack Skills — Integration Intelligence No Other Tool Provides

Individual skills cover single libraries. But developers don't use libraries in isolation. They use *stacks*.

Your project runs Next.js + better-auth + SpacetimeDB + Serwist. Each library has its own API. But the interesting knowledge is in the *integrations*: when you modify the auth flow, you need to update the Serwist service worker cache exclusion. When you change a database schema, the auth session type needs to match.

No individual skill captures this. Stack skills do.

```
@Ferris SS
```

Ferris reads your project's dependency manifests, ranks libraries by significance (import frequency, not just presence in package.json), presents the scope for confirmation, then runs parallel extraction across your stack. But here's the key step: after extracting individual APIs, Ferris scans for **co-import patterns** — places where two or more libraries interact in the same file or module.

The output:

```
skills/my-project-stack/
  SKILL.md              -- Integration patterns + project conventions
  context-snippet.md    -- Compressed stack index
  metadata.json         -- Component versions, integration graph
  references/
    nextjs.md           -- Project-specific subset (not generic docs)
    better-auth.md
    integrations/
      auth-db.md        -- Cross-library pattern
      pwa-auth.md       -- Cross-library pattern
```

The `integrations/` directory is where the magic lives. Each file documents a specific cross-library pattern with provenance citations tracing to your actual code. Your agent now knows: "When you modify the auth flow, update the Serwist cache exclusion at `src/sw.ts:L23`."

This isn't information the agent could hallucinate. It's structural truth extracted from your codebase.

---

## The Ecosystem Play

SKF doesn't exist in isolation. It produces skills that plug directly into the [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io) ecosystem.

**Three ownership levels:**

- **Official** — Library maintainers generate skills for their own code. Provenance verifies authorship. Distributed via `npx skills publish`.
- **Internal** — Teams generate skills for internal services. Shipped in the repo's `skills/` directory.
- **Community** — Developers generate skills for dependencies they don't own. Marked accordingly. Used locally.

The vision: library maintainers ship skills alongside their npm packages. When a consumer installs `better-auth@4.0.0`, the matching skill is one command away:

```bash
npx skills add owner/repo --skill better-auth
```

Breaking changes? Run `@Ferris AS` to detect drift, `@Ferris US` to regenerate while preserving your manual additions, `@Ferris TS` to verify completeness. The skill lifecycle runs alongside your development lifecycle — like tests, like linting, like CI.

---

## The Confidence Model — Trust, But Verify

SKF doesn't ask you to trust it. It shows its work.

Every claim carries a confidence tier:

- **T1** — AST extraction. Structurally verified from the parsed syntax tree. The gold standard. Available in Forge and Deep modes.
- **T2** — QMD evidence. Mined from indexed documentation, changelogs, issues, and PRs. Rich context, strong evidence. Available in Deep mode.
- **T3** — External documentation. Fetched from remote sources. Quarantined as untrusted.

When sources disagree, higher tiers win for instructions. Lower tiers are preserved as annotations — never discarded, but clearly marked as lower confidence.

The provenance map (`forge-data/{name}/provenance-map.json`) records every extraction. Another developer can reproduce the same skill from the same commit. The build is deterministic. The evidence is auditable.

This is what "zero hallucination tolerance" means in practice. Not "we try really hard." But: every instruction has a source, and you can verify it.

---

## Your Code. Your Skills. Your Agent. Full Control.

SKF is opinionated about one thing: accuracy. Everything else is yours to control.

- **Your code stays local.** All processing happens on your machine. AST analysis, QMD indexing, spec validation — nothing leaves your environment. The `doc_fetcher` warns before contacting external services.
- **Your skills are transparent.** Every instruction cites its source. Every metadata.json records the commit, tier, and coverage stats. No black boxes.
- **Your workflow is flexible.** Quick mode for speed. Forge mode for truth. Deep mode for intelligence. Upgrade when ready.
- **Your manual additions survive.** Mark sections with `[MANUAL]` and `@Ferris US` (update) preserves them during regeneration. Your expertise coexists with automated extraction.
- **Your agent context is managed.** The CLAUDE.md managed section updates on export, not on every draft. You control when your agent's context changes.

---

## Try It Now

Three commands to your first skill (requires [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/) >= 22):

```bash
# Install the module
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install

# Setup your forge
@Ferris SF

# Generate a skill for any library
@Ferris QS https://github.com/some-org/some-library
```

Your agent stops hallucinating that library. Forty-seven seconds.

Want the full pipeline? Brief it, create it, test it, export it:

```
@Ferris BS    # Scope and design
@Ferris CS    # Compile from brief
@Ferris TS    # Verify completeness
@Ferris EX    # Package and inject into CLAUDE.md
```

Want your entire project stack understood? One command:

```
@Ferris SS    # Stack skill with integration intelligence
```

**Already using the [BMad Method](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD)?** You can install SKF as a custom module during `npx bmad-method install` — select "Add custom modules" and point to the SKF `src/` folder. Or add it to an existing BMad project anytime with `npx bmad-module-skill-forge install`.

**Links:**

- **GitHub:** [armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge)
- **Documentation:** [Architecture deep-dive](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/architecture/)
- **agentskills.io:** [Specification](https://agentskills.io/specification) | [What are skills?](https://agentskills.io/what-are-skills)
- **BMAD Method:** [bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD)

---

*Built in 10 days with AI agents. 71 traceable decisions. Zero hallucination tolerance.*

*Your knowledge, verified and compiled, so your agent never starts from zero again.*

lines: -Breaking changes? Run `@Ferris AS` to detect drift, `@Ferris US` to regenerate while preserving your manual additions, `@Ferris TS` to verify completeness. The skill lifecycle runs alongside your development lifecycle — like tests, like linting, like CI.

+Breaking changes? Run `@Ferris AS` to detect drift, `@Ferris US` to regenerate while preserving your manual additions, `@Ferris TS` to verify completeness — with external validators like [skill-check](https://github.com/thedaviddias/skill-check) for spec compliance and [tessl](https://tessl.io) for content quality scoring. The skill lifecycle runs alongside your development lifecycle — like tests, like linting, like CI.
